**collaboration_potential**: High (**FLAG: Direct Collaborator**).
This is the most ambitious institutional effort to build a literal Spaceship Earth digital twin [cite: 53, 55].
**governance_model**: Led and coordinated by the European Commission (DG CNECT), implemented by entrusted scientific entities [cite: 52, 53, 56]
**open_source_tools**: DestinE Platform (cloud-based architecture), Digital Twin Engine [cite: 53, 54]
**community_size**: >3,500 users on the platform since June 2024 launch [cite: 55]

### What They Do
Destination Earth is an ambitious European Union initiative aiming to create a highly accurate, dynamic digital replica—a digital twin—of the Earth by 2030 [cite: 52, 55].
The project harnesses high-performance computing (HPC), climate models, Earth Observation (EO) data from Copernicus, and machine learning to simulate natural phenomena, hazards, and human activities [cite: 53, 54, 55].
DestinE is divided among three major European agencies.
ECMWF implements the Digital Twin Engine and the first two digital twins: the Climate Change Adaptation twin (multidecadal simulations) and the Weather-Induced Extremes twin (high-resolution forecasting for floods and droughts) [cite: 54, 55, 56].
ESA manages the user-friendly DestinE Platform, while EUMETSAT builds the massive Data Lake that processes big data in the cloud [cite: 53, 54].
The system allows scientists and policymakers to interactively test adaptation strategies and anticipate the socioeconomic impacts of climate change to support the EU's goal of carbon neutrality by 2050 [cite: 53, 55].
### Why They Matter for Planetary Governance Prototyping
DestinE is the zenith of the Spaceship Earth concept.
While grassroots Web3 networks build the financial and governance layers, DestinE is building the actual physics engine of the planet [cite: 53, 55].
Integrating DestinE's predictive models into a planetary governance simulation would allow players to test the exact outcomes of their policy decisions (e.g., carbon taxes, infrastructure investments) against a state-of-the-art supercomputer simulation [cite: 54, 55].
## Spatial Web Foundation / Verses AI

**Name:** Spatial Web Foundation / Verses AI
**Type:** Company / Foundation
**Orientation:** SPACESHIP
**Website:** https://www.verses.ai/
**Location:** Global (HQ Vancouver, BC)
**Scale:** Large enterprise and institutional partnerships
**Maturity:** Standardized by IEEE in May 2025

### Key People
Gabriel René, CEO of VERSES [cite: 57]
Capm Petersen, Chief Innovation Officer, VERSES [cite: 58]

### Partners and Collaborators
Institute of Electrical and Electronics Engineers (IEEE) [cite: 57, 59, 60]
NASA Jet Propulsion Laboratory (JPL) [cite: 58]
California State University, Northridge (CSUN) [cite: 58]
Analog [cite: 61]
Dentons US [cite: 61]
Open Geospatial Consortium (OGC) [cite: 60]

### Domain-Specific Fields
**funding_sources**: Publicly traded company (CBOE: VERS, OTCQB: VRSSF) [cite: 57, 58]
**key_projects**: Genius™ platform, Digital Twins Interoperability project with NASA JPL, IEEE P2874 Spatial Web Standards [cite: 57, 58, 60]
**partner_orgs**: IEEE, NASA JPL, CSUN, Analog, Dentons US [cite: 57, 58, 61]
**event_participation**: The Future of AI Governance Webinar Series [cite: 61]
**publications**: "The Future of AI Governance" (joint paper with Dentons) [cite: 59, 61]
**collaboration_potential**: High (**FLAG: Direct Collaborator**).
Building the underlying protocols to make physical space programmable with digital rules [cite: 57, 60].
**governance_model**: Corporate governance combined with open, public standards development via the non-profit Spatial Web Foundation [cite: 58, 60]
**open_source_tools**: IEEE P2874 Spatial Web Standards, Hyperspatial Modeling Language (HSML), Hyperspatial Transaction Protocol (HSTP) [cite: 57, 59, 60]
**community_size**: 100+ global working group members assisting standards development [cite: 60]

### What They Do
VERSES AI, in conjunction with the non-profit Spatial Web Foundation, is pioneering the "Spatial Web"—a fundamental shift that creates a World Wide Web for the physical world [cite: 57].
They championed the creation of the IEEE P2874 Spatial Web Standards, which outline the Hyperspatial Modeling Language (HSML) and Hyperspatial Transaction Protocol (HSTP) [cite: 57, 59, 60].
These protocols provide a universal language that allows artificial intelligence, humans, and machines to interoperate safely within physical and digital spaces [cite: 58, 60].
VERSES' flagship product, Genius™, uses active inference (based on Professor Karl Friston's Free Energy Principle) to create AI systems that understand context rather than relying on massive historical data scraping [cite: 59, 60].
Recently, VERSES successfully demonstrated cross-platform digital twin interoperability with NASA JPL, allowing distributed platforms to collaborate on a real-time simulation of a lunar rover using spatial web protocols [cite: 58].
### Why They Matter for Planetary Governance Prototyping
If a planetary simulation requires translating physical actions into digital tracking, the Spatial Web is the required infrastructure [cite: 57, 60].
HSML allows the physical world to become programmable, meaning governance rules, smart contracts, and ecological constraints can be embedded directly into physical locations [cite: 59, 60].
In The Garden, players could use HSML to assign property rights, emissions caps, or ecological standing to specific GPS coordinates, allowing autonomous AI agents to enforce planetary governance rules automatically in the real world [cite: 60, 61].
